It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board didn't change at all! Clicking at (31, 22) didn't change anything. This means I clicked on the wrong cell - probably the green square's boundary or outside of it.
Let me find the exact center of the right green square by looking at its boundary points.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Find exact positions of green squares
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Green: boundary={b}, min_row={min(rows)}, max_row={max(rows)}, min_col={min(cols)}, max_col={max(cols)}")
        for child_id in n['children']:
            for cn in seg['nodes']:
                if cn['id'] == child_id:
                    cb = cn['boundary']
                    crows = [p[0] for p in cb]
                    ccols = [p[1] for p in cb]
                    print(f"  dot: color={cn['color']}, boundary={cb}, center=({sum(crows)//len(crows)},{sum(ccols)//len(ccols)})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Green: boundary=[[30, 21], [30, 23], [32, 23], [32, 21]], min_row=30, max_row=32, min_col=21, max_col=23
  dot: color=W, boundary=[[31, 22]], center=(31,22)
Green: boundary=[[33, 12], [33, 14], [35, 14], [35, 12]], min_row=33, max_row=35, min_col=12, max_col=14
  dot: color=c, boundary=[[34, 13]], center=(34,13)

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so the right green square is at rows 30-32, cols 21-23, with the dot at (31, 22). I clicked at (31, 22) and nothing changed. That's strange - I should have clicked on the dot.
Wait, the result said `board_changed: false`. Maybe clicking on the dot doesn't change it when the green square is not in a frame? Or maybe the click didn't register because of some other reason.
Let me try clicking on the center of the green square itself, not the dot. The green square's center is (31, 22), which is the same as the dot's position.
Actually, looking at the boundary, the green square is 3x3 (rows 30-32, cols 21-23), and the dot is at (31, 22), which is the center. So clicking at (31, 22) should click on the dot.
But the board didn't change. Maybe the green square needs to be in a frame for the dot color to change? Let me check if the left green square's dot color changed when I clicked on it while it was in the frame.
Actually, I've been clicking on the left green square while it's in the frame, and the dot color has been changing. So the frame shouldn't matter.
Wait, maybe the issue is that I clicked at (31, 22) but the green square has moved since then? Let me check the current state.
Actually, the segmentation output shows the green square is still at (31, 22) with a W dot. So the click didn't change anything.
Let me try clicking on a different cell within the green square, like (31, 21) or (31, 23).
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. When I clicked on the green square before, I was clicking on the center (31, 31) when it was at that position, and the dot color changed. Now I'm clicking on (31, 22) and nothing changes. Maybe the green square needs to be in a specific position for the dot color to change?
Or maybe there's a timing issue - the click needs to happen at a specific step?
Let me try clicking on the left green square at (34, 13) to see if the dot color still changes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the left green square to see if dot color still changes
# Left green square dot is at (34, 13)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 13}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
